title: String Traversal

project_usage: Parsing user input character-by-character to sanitize inputs
Building lexical analyzers (lexers) in compiler design

glossary.definition: When a program wastes large amounts of RAM by creating unnecessary copies of data.
Text data that cannot be changed once created; any edit requires building a completely new copy.
A data container designed to let you safely change its contents in place.

overview.what: String traversal is the process of examining or transforming a string character-by-character in a single linear pass. Since strings are essentially arrays of characters, traversal involves moving a cursor across the sequence and updating a state based on the current character.
overview.why: Naive string manipulation often relies on chained high-level methods (e.g., splitting, replacing, joining) which can cause severe memory bloat and degrade performance to O(N^2) due to repeated memory allocations. A single-pass O(N) traversal requires minimal, constant auxiliary memory.
where_used: UTF-8 decoders parsing byte streams into characters
Validating formatting or executing simple character transformations

why_learning_this: It forms the foundational building block for all advanced string algorithms.
Interviewers look for candidates who avoid unnecessary string copies in languages with immutable strings.
Understanding state-tracking during traversal is critical for parsing logic.

common_mistakes.title: Repeated string concatenation
common_mistakes.explanation: In languages like Java or Python, strings are immutable. Doing `str += char` inside a loop creates a brand new string in memory every single time, leading to O(N^2) time complexity. You must use a mutable structure like a StringBuilder or an array instead.

common_mistakes.title: Unnecessary multiple passes
common_mistakes.explanation: Calling `.replace()`, then `.lower()`, then `.strip()` traverses the entire string three separate times. A single manual traversal can do all three in one O(N) pass.

recall_questions.q: Why is string traversal fundamentally O(N) time?
recall_questions.answer: Because the algorithm must visit every single character in the sequence exactly once to evaluate the entire string.

recall_questions.q: Why is it dangerous to repeatedly concatenate strings inside a loop?
recall_questions.answer: Immutable strings require reallocating and copying the entire string every time a character is added, degrading an O(N) loop into an O(N^2) operation.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary structural invariant of a standard linear traversal?
recall_questions.answer: At step i, all characters before index i have been completely processed and their implications are captured in the current state variables.

understanding_checks.question: If you need to parse a massive 10GB log file and count the number of error flags, why is a manual line-by-line traversal better than calling `file.read().split('\n')`?
understanding_checks.answer: Calling `read().split()` attempts to load the entire 10GB file into RAM simultaneously, crashing the program. A manual line-by-line traversal only keeps a single line in memory at any given time (O(1) auxiliary space).
understanding_checks.why: This demonstrates the difference between high-level abstractions that prioritize developer convenience and low-level traversal that prioritize memory constraints.

understanding_checks.question: You are traversing a string to check if it contains valid brackets. You maintain a `balance` integer, incrementing on `(` and decrementing on `)`. What must be true about this state variable for the string to be valid?
understanding_checks.answer: The `balance` must never drop below 0 at any point during the traversal, and it must exactly equal 0 at the very end.
understanding_checks.why: This is a classic state-tracking pattern. It proves that you cannot just count the total number of brackets; the state at every individual step of the traversal matters.

aha_moment.prediction: The first method is simpler and just as fast.
aha_moment.common_guess: String concatenation is a basic O(1) operation in modern languages.
aha_moment.why: Strings are immutable arrays. You cannot append to them. You have to allocate a entirely new array and copy the old data over. The array-join method allocates a dynamic array once and writes into it, maintaining true linear time.

practice_tasks.title: Count specific characters
practice_tasks.prompt: Write a function that traverses a string in a single pass and returns the count of vowels.
practice_tasks.solution: Initialize a counter to 0. Loop through `s`. If the character is in 'aeiouAEIOU', increment the counter. Return the counter.
